coredata.dc:title: In situ formation of absorbance agents for ascorbic acid detection using SnO2/carbon dots nanozyme with a wide detection range
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhichao
Liu, Xinghao
Chen, Tongtong
Wang, Zhixuan
Xie, Zheng
coredata.dc:description: The strong reducing ability of ascorbic acid (AA) usually resulted in a limited linear detection range. To address the defect, a colorimetric sensor was constructed using an in-situ generated absorbance indicator. The SnO2/carbon dots (Sn-CDs) displayed high peroxidase-like (POD) activity, which could oxidize o-phenylenediamine (oPD) to ox-oPD (450 nm), and the absorbance at 450 nm decreased as the concentration of AA increased. The dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) formed by the oxidation of AA further reacted with oPD to produce 3-(1,2-dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ, at 336 nm absorbance). And the absorbance at 336 nm increased as the concentration of AA increased. Based on the two signals, the DFQ signal offered two broader detection ranges (5.62–131.03 μM and 2.84–28.39 mM) and a lower limit of detection (0.88 μM) than the ox-oPD signal (16.54–51.62 μM, 6.88 μM). And the colorimetric sensor based on 336 nm DFQ signal exhibited a higher anti-interfering ability in complex environments. In the practical samples (Vitamin C tablets, orange, and lemon), the concentrations of AA detected by colorimetric sensor was close to that detected by high-performance liquid chromatography, which indicated that constructed sensor exhibited superior precision and practicality. The study not only presented a valuable tool for the detection of AA, but also offered a novel strategy for highly selective colorimetric sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Ascorbic acid
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physical and chemical characterization of Sn-CDs nanozyme
POD-like activity and kinetics parameters of Sn-CDs nanozyme
Determination of AA in real samples
Colorimetric detection of AA and mechanism
Detection of AA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of Sn-CDs nanozyme synthesis process (a); Characterization of Sn-CDs nanozyme; XRD patterns (b); Wide (c) and narrow (d) range of Raman spectra; TEM image (e); Particle size distribution (f); High-resolution TEM image (g).

FT-IR spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (a); XPS wide spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (b); Elemental composition determined by XPS (c); C 1s spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (d); N 1s spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (e); O 1s spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (f); Sn 3d spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (g); The UV–Vis and FL emission (Ex = 540 nm) spectrum of Sn-CDs nanozyme (h); FL emission spectra of Sn-CDs nanozyme under 500, 540, and 580 nm excitation (i).

POD-like activity test, UV–Vis absorption spectra of H2O2, Sn-CDs, oPD, H2O2 + Sn-CDs, H2O2 + oPD, Sn-CDs + oPD, and Sn-CDs + H2O2 + oPD (a); ESR spectrum of the formation of hydroxyl radical (b); Steady-state kinetic assay of Sn-CDs nanozyme for H2O2 (c), and corresponding double reciprocal diagram (d); Steady-state kinetic assay of Sn-CDs nanozyme for oPD (e), and corresponding double reciprocal diagram (f).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of different AA concentrations ranging from 5.62 μM to 18.93 mM (a); Calibration curve for AA detection at 450 nm (b); Calibration curve for detecting relatively lower concentrations of AA at 336 nm (c); Calibration curve of detecting relatively higher concentrations of AA at 336 nm (d).

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Sn-CDs + AA, Sn-CDs + oPD + H2O2, and Sn-CDs + oPD + H2O2 + AA (a); UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oPD + H2O2, oPD + AA and oPD + H2O2 + AA (b); The oxidation reaction of oPD and the reduction reaction of ox-oPD by AA (c); The Schiff base reaction between oPD and DHA forming a new DFQ (d). Selectivity of Sn-CDs nanozyme for AA detection at 450 nm using various interfering agents (Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Glu, GSH, DA, AA, and Gly at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1) (e); The selectivity of Sn-CDs nanozyme for AA detection at 336 nm (Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Glu, GSH, DA, AA, and Gly at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1) (f).
Determination of AA content in Vitamin C tablets, orange, and lemon.

head.ce:title: In situ formation of absorbance agents for ascorbic acid detection using SnO2/carbon dots nanozyme with a wide detection range

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of AA detection by two absorbance positions by Sn-CDs.

The strong reducing ability of ascorbic acid (AA) usually resulted in a limited linear detection range. To address the defect, a colorimetric sensor was constructed using an in-situ generated absorbance indicator. The SnO2/carbon dots (Sn-CDs) displayed high peroxidase-like (POD) activity, which could oxidize o-phenylenediamine (oPD) to ox-oPD (450 nm), and the absorbance at 450 nm decreased as the concentration of AA increased. The dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) formed by the oxidation of AA further reacted with oPD to produce 3-(1,2-dihydroxyethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ, at 336 nm absorbance). And the absorbance at 336 nm increased as the concentration of AA increased. Based on the two signals, the DFQ signal offered two broader detection ranges (5.62–131.03 μM and 2.84–28.39 mM) and a lower limit of detection (0.88 μM) than the ox-oPD signal (16.54–51.62 μM, 6.88 μM). And the colorimetric sensor based on 336 nm DFQ signal exhibited a higher anti-interfering ability in complex environments. In the practical samples (Vitamin C tablets, orange, and lemon), the concentrations of AA detected by colorimetric sensor was close to that detected by high-performance liquid chromatography, which indicated that constructed sensor exhibited superior precision and practicality. The study not only presented a valuable tool for the detection of AA, but also offered a novel strategy for highly selective colorimetric sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) was an important water-soluble vitamin found widely in fresh vegetables and fruits. And it was pivotal in numerous metabolic processes within organisms. Abnormal AA concentrations could be a signal for some diseases, such as scurvy, cancers, and cardiovascular disease [1]. Therefore, the quantitative detection of AA in different media was highly significant. At present, the detection methods for AA included electrochemical, chemical titration, liquid chromatography, and fluorimetric [2–5]. The methods had successfully detected AA with high sensitivity and accuracy, but they frequently encountered some challenges, such as highly trained personnel, complex operations, and expensive instruments.

Colorimetric assay was one of the most promising methods for detection due to its ease of operation and inexpensive devices. In comparison to natural enzymes, nanozyme-based colorimetric sensors exhibit advantages of high stability and relatively low cost. In particular, nanozyme-based colorimetric sensors had drawn significant attention because of the specific reaction catalyzed by nanozymes which could catalyze the substrates to produce a color production. For example, the substrates such as 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (oPD), and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) could be oxidized to forms, ox-TMB, ox-oPD, and ox-ABTS, which displayed new peaks at 650 nm, 415 nm, and 420 nm, respectively [6]. By far, there were more than 1200 types of nanozymes [7]. Carbon dots (CDs), one of them was an optimal nanomaterial for creating nanozyme-based sensing platforms, thanks to their superior optical properties, ease of synthesis and modification, and high stability [8]. A colorimetric sensor based on Fe3O4 nanozyme was developed for detection after discovery of Fe3O4 with peroxidase-like (POD) activity [9,10]. Then the colorimetric sensor based on CDs nanozyme was established. And the sensor were applied to detect various substances, including AA [11], H2O2 [12], glucose [13], pesticide [14], and Hg2+ [15].

The AA with four –OH groups could reduce the blue ox-TMB to colorless TMB. Hence, a sensor was constructed to detect AA based on the change of color at 652 nm. For example, a colorimetric sensor based on POD-like Mn-CDs nanozyme exhibited a linear range for AA detection from 0.05 to 2.5 μM [11]. When Co9S8 was coupled with CDs, the detection range extended to 0.5 to 20 μM [16]. However, the aforementioned colorimetric method frequently suffered from a restricted detection range because of the high reductive capacity of AA. To expand the detection range, a new signal from the nanozyme system was required for advancement. In the nanozyme system with oPD as substrate, the oPD could be oxidized to ox-oPD. Many colorimetric sensors were established based on the absorbance signal of the ox-oPD. Interestingly, the AA was introduced into the nanozyme system with oPD, and a new fluorescence emission at 440 nm was exhibited [17]. Because the AA was first oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) which then reacted with oPD to generate the 3-(1,2-dihydroxy ethyl) furo [3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ). And based on the new signal, a fluorometric method achieves a linear range for AA detection from 0.1 to 25 μM [18]. This signal did not rely on the typical signal of ox-oPD, which might provide a way to expand the detection range of highly reductive AA. Based on literature research, there was no report on using the absorbance of DFQ to construct a colorimetric sensor in nanozyme system. Therefore, the new absorbance detection of AA using DFQ was an ideal method.

In this study, a colorimetric sensor for AA detection was developed based on the POD-like Sn-CDs nanozyme. The Sn-CDs were synthesized via the pyrolysis method and demonstrated POD-like activity. When combined with H2O2 and oPD, Sn-CDs nanozyme could catalyze the reaction with AA to ultimately form the in-situ generated DFQ with absorption at 336 nm. As AA concentrations increased, the signal of DFQ at 336 nm increased. Furthermore, a colorimetric sensing platform for AA detection was developed. Due to the specific Schiff base reaction between DHA and oPD, the colorimetric sensor achieved high selectivity for AA. Therefore, the method provided a new approach for detecting AA with a wide detection range up to the millimolar per liter level.

O-phenylenediamine (oPD), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), and tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O) were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 30 % hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·4H2O), and ammonium acetate (CH3COONH4) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc·3H2O), ethanol, and acetate (HAc) were obtained from Sinopharm (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C tablets were obtained from Northeast Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Lemons and oranges were obtained from a local supermarket. The solutions were prepared using distilled water. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The synthesis of Sn-CDs nanozyme was via solvent-free pyrolysis method [19]. In brief, 0.2 g oPD and 0.6 g SnCl4·5H2O were mixed, which was transferred into 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reactor was then placed in the oven and heated to 200°C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid products were collected and washed with ethanol and water three times. Finally, the products were dried overnight at 60°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical characterization of Sn-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D8 Advance instrument (Bruker, Germany), with data collected over a range of 10° to 80°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Raman spectroscopy was performed on a Horiba Raman (Horiba, Japan) instrument with 532 nm excitation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on Nicolet™ iS50 (Thermo, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy was carried out on a Spark microplate reader (Tecan, Austria) and UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence studies were performed on a Fluolog®-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on AXIS SUPRA (Kyatos, England). High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) was carried out on a Prominence LC-20A instrument (Shimadzu, Japan). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted on EMXplus-6/1 (Bruker, Germany).

The UV–Vis spectrum ranged from 250 nm to 800 nm of Sn-CDs nanozyme in ethanol at a concentration of 10 μg mL−1 was detected by the UV-2700 spectrophotometer. And the photoluminescence spectra were detected by the Fluolog®-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the condition of 1 mg mL−1 Sn-CDs nanozyme ethanol solution ranged from 560 nm to 800 nm. The PL quantum yield (PLQY) was obtained from Fluorolog®-3 instrument equipped with a quantum φ integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like activity and kinetics parameters of Sn-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The POD-like activity of Sn-CDs nanozyme was assessed [20]. 200 μL solutions of Sn-CDs nanozyme (50 μg mL−1), oPD (50 μg mL−1), and H2O2 (1 mM) were added into a 96-well plate. And the mixed solutions of (Sn-CDs nanozyme + oPD, Sn-CDs nanozyme + H2O2, oPD + H2O2, and Sn-CDs nanozyme + oPD + H2O2) were tested at the same concentration. Absorbance was recorded in the range of 400 to 500 nm by the microplate reader.

For the kinetic experiment, 40 μL of Sn-CDs nanozyme (1 mg mL−1), 100 μL of oPD (10 mg mL−1), 800 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.7), and different concentrations of H2O2 solution (1.22–16.67 mM, divided into 6 concentration gradients) were mixed in 96-well plate. Then the plate was tested in the microplate reader for 20 min, and the UV–vis spectrum at 400–500 nm was recorded every 1 min. The test for oPD solutions (2.26–38.08 mM, divided into 7 concentration gradients) were tested at the same condition, with 100 μL of H2O2 (0.5 M). The Michaelis-Menten equation (Eq. (1) and Lineweaver-Burk equation (Eq. (2) were utilized to determine kinetic parameters including the Michaelis constant (Km ) and maximum reaction velocity (Vmax ).
(1)
v =
V
max
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]

=

1

V
max

200 μL solutions of Sn-CDs nanozyme (50 μg mL−1), oPD (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (1 mM), and NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.7) were added into a 96-well plate. Then, different AA concentrations ranged from 5.62 μM to 18.93 mM was added into the solution. The mixed solution was placed in an oven at 37 °C for 15 min. Finally, the absorbance spectrum was recorded at 300 to 500 nm.

The hydroxyl radicals (•OH) trapping experiments were validated by ESR spectroscopy. DMPO as a spin trap reagent, could react with •OH to form stable spin adducts. The measurements were as follows: 100 μL of Sn-CDs solution (1 mg mL−1) and 100 μL of H2O2 (0.5 M) were mixed with 800 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.7). The 30 μL of DMPO (100 mM) was added to the 30 μL aforementioned solution., which was tested after 2 min reaction. For comparison, another experiment was conducted with the same parameters without the addition of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The Vitamin C tablets were ground into powder for further use. 10 μL (0.1 mg mL−1) Vitamin C tablet solution was added in the 200 μL solutions of Sn-CDs nanozyme (50 μg mL−1), oPD (50 μg mL−1), H2O2 (1 mM), and NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.7), then the mixed solution was kept in an oven at 37°C for 15 min. The absorbance spectrum was recorded ranged from 300 to 400 nm. The orange and lemon samples were treated before analysis. In details, the orange and lemon were cut into small pieces which were added to a NaAc-HAc buffer solution at a weight ratio of 0.5 g mL−1. The mixed solutions were sonicated for 30 min and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove suspended solids. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for analysis. For the HPLC method, the separations were carried out on a Carbon 18 (C18) column of 250 mm * 4.6 mm (Elite, Dalian, China). The mobile phase was a mixture of 20 mM KH2PO4 – methanol (95/5, v/v) with a pH of 4.0. The flow rate and column temperature were kept at 1.0 mL min−1 and 30°C, respectively. The detection wavelength was 245 nm with injected volume of 20 μL.

Fig. 1 (a) showed the process for the fabrication of Sn-CDs nanozyme. The Sn-CDs nanozyme was obtained by the classical solvent-free pyrolysis method. The crystalline structure of prepared Sn-CDs nanozyme was investigated via XRD analysis, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The two relatively broad peaks at 25-30° and 40-44° correspond to the (002) and (101) planes of graphitic structured carbon materials, which indicated that the graphitization degree of the carbon nucleus of Sn-CDs nanozyme was low. Additionally, the high-intensity peaks at 26.5°, 33.8°, 37.9°, 51.6°, 54.5°, 57.8°, 61.7°, 65.6°, 71.0°, and 78.5° were attributed to the (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (002), (310), (301), (202), and (321) planes of SnO2, respectively. It was matched with the PDF card number 41–1445, confirming the presence of SnO2 in the Sn-CDs nanozyme [21]. Raman spectroscopy was performed to analyze the structure of Sn-CDs. The Raman spectrum was shown in Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(d). The peaks at 1366 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 correspond to the D (Defect) peak and G (Graphite) peak, respectively. The intensity of D peak was higher than that of the G peak, which indicated that the crystallinity of CDs in Sn-CDs nanozyme was relatively low. And the Raman shift at 492.2 cm−1 and 614.2 cm−1 correspond to the A1g and Eg modes of crystalline SnO2 [22]. The results indicated the coexistence of a low degree of graphitization of CDs and high crystalline SnO2, which was consistent with the XRD results. Although the basic structure and generative mechanism of CDs were not clear so far [23], the carbon core of Sn-CDs nanozyme consisted of less sp2 -hybridized crystalline forms with more sp3 -hybridized amorphous carbon structures by the XRD and Raman results. Furthermore, the structure and morphology of Sn-CDs were characterized by TEM. The prepared Sn-CDs nanozyme exhibited a spherical morphology (Fig. 1(e)), and the average particle size of CDs was about 4.96 nm (Fig. 1(f)). In addition, a crystal lattice with a spacing of 0.21 nm was consistent with the crystal face of (101) of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1(g)), indicating that the Sn-CDs nanozyme were composed of nanocrystalline carbon cores with the sp2 hybridized of carbon atoms [24]. The high-resolution TEM images of Sn-CDs also showed good crystallinity (Fig. S1 (a) and (b)), their crystal plane spacing was measured at 0.339 nm and 0.269 nm, specifically the (110) and (101) planes in the SnO2 lattice (Fig. S1 (c) and (d)). In short, the Sn-CDs nanozyme was composed of a carbon core with a low degree of graphitization and highly crystalline SnO2.

The surface states including functional groups and chemical states of the Sn-CDs nanozyme were analyzed by FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the peaks at 609 and 648 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of Sn-O [25]. The broader band at ∼ 3390 cm−1 was assigned to the N-H and O-H stretching vibrations. The stretching vibrations peak located at 2921 cm−1 corresponded to the typical C-H bonds. The strong vibrations peaks situated at 1582 to 1618 cm−1 were attributed to the C=O and C=N bonds. The bands at 1147 cm−1 and 1322 to 1353 cm−1 were ascribed to the C-O and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively. The sharp peaks located at 1475 to 1525 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=C bonds [19]. The results showed that the functional groups of N-H, O-H, C=O, and C=N existed on the surface of Sn-CDs nanozyme. The specific location of these bonds suggested that the Sn-CDs nanozyme consists of a sp2 carbon core structure with oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Furthermore, the XPS full survey of the Sn-CDs nanozyme showed peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Cl 2p, and Sn 3d (Fig. 2(b)), these peaks were located at binding energies of 284.8 eV, 399.5 eV, 531.3 eV, 197.8 eV, and 487.2 eV, respectively. The elemental ratios of C, N, O, Cl, and Sn in the Sn-CDs nanozyme were determined to be 68.49 %, 16.23 %, 8.02 %, 4.74 %, and 2.51 %, respectively (Fig. 2(c)).

The high-resolution C 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.3 eV corresponding to C-C, C-O, or C-N, and C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2(d)). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum showed three peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.4 eV, assigned to the pyridinic N, amine N, and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 2(e)) [22], which indicated that the N atom was successfully doped in Sn-CDs nanozyme, and the main occurrence of nitrogen was in nitrogen-containing heterocycles.

The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) revealed two peaks located at 531.6 eV and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the C=O and C-O bonds, respectively. The XPS results were consistent with FT-IR. In addition, the two peaks at binding energies of 487.6 eV and 496.0 eV normally represented the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 (Fig. 2(g)) [25], and it suggested that the Sn element in Sn-CDs nanozyme was tetravalent. The FT-IR and XPS results confirm that N and Cl were successfully doped into the Sn-CDs nanozyme. The surface states of Sn-CDs nanozyme primarily consisted of polyaromatic structures containing oxygen and nitrogen, with the presence of N-H, O-H, C=O, and C=N functional groups. The abundant functional groups on the Sn-CDs nanozyme surface might favor its optical properties and catalytic activity. Subsequently, the optical properties and nanozyme activity were evaluated.

To verify the optical properties of Sn-CDs nanozyme, both UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded (Fig. 2(h)). The Sn-CDs nanozyme showed a high-intensity peak located at 284 nm in ethanol solution, which was assigned to the π-π* transitions of C=N and C=C bonds. The relatively lower intensity peak located at 425 nm was attributed to n-π* transitions of C=O bonds [26]. The broad absorbance bands between 480 and 600 nm corresponded to the surface states [27]. For the fluorescence spectra, the Sn-CDs nanozyme exhibited near-infrared fluorescence emission at 650 nm and 710 nm under 540 nm excitation (Fig. 2(h)). In addition, the emission remained unchanged at the different excitation wavelengths ranged from 500 to 580 nm, whih indicated that the Sn-CDs nanozyme did not display the excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. 2(i)). The absolute PLQY of the Sn-CDs nanozyme was 18.9 % (Fig. S2).

To evaluate the POD-like activity of Sn-CDs nanozyme, oPD was used as the substrate with the assistance of H2O2. In the nanozyme system, the POD-like Sn-CDs nanozyme could catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH, which then catalyzed the substrate oPD to form ox-oPD with an absorbance at 450 nm. The absorbance at 450 nm served as the signal of the POD-like activity of the Sn-CDs nanozyme. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the system of Sn-CDs, oPD, H2O2, Sn-CDs + H2O2, and Sn-CDs + oPD showed no absorbance peak at 450 nm. With the addition of H2O2 to oPD, the system of oPD + H2O2 showed a weak intensity at 450 nm [28]. In the system of Sn-CDs + oPD + H2O2, absorbance intensity at 450 nm was stronger than other systems, which might be caused by the generated high activity species (•OH). To validate the conclusion, the ESR experiment was conducted. As shown in Fig. 3(b), in the Sn-CDs + DMPO system, there was no obvious peak. And in the Sn-CDs + DMPO + H2O2 system, a typical four-line ESR pattern with the intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 indicated that the Sn-CDs + oPD + H2O2 system could generate •OH. The above results approved that the prepared Sn-CDs nanozyme possessed excellent POD-like activity. Furthermore, the POD-like activity of Sn-CDs nanozyme was influenced by pH and temperature. The effects of pH and temperature on the activity of Sn-CDs nanozyme were further detected. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the absorbance at 450 nm was decreased as the pH increased from 3.7 to 5.6. These results indicated that the Sn-CDs nanozyme showed higher catalytic activity in more acidic NaAc-HAc solutions, which was in line with the previous findings on CDs nanozymes [16,29]. Fig. S3(b) showed the temperature effects on catalytic activity, and the absorbance at 450 nm increased with the temperature from 4 to 55°C. The Sn-CDs nanozyme exhibited higher catalytic activity in higher temperatures, indicating that the POD-like activity was temperature-dependent [30].

To further assess the kinetic parameters of POD-like activity, the absorbance of ox-oPD at 450 nm was monitored over time. According to the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk equations (Eqs.1 and 2), the Km and Vmax could be obtained. As shown in Fig. 3(c), when the concentration of oPD remained constant, the reaction rate accelerated at lower H2O2 concentrations and slowed down at higher H2O2 concentrations. Furthermore, the Km and Vmax values for H2O2 were 8.59 mM and 1.41*10-8 M s− 1, respectively (Fig. 3(d)). When the H2O2 concentration was fixed, the oPD concentration was varied, and a typical nanozyme reaction process curve was obtained (Fig. 3(e)). The variation trend of the reaction process curve for oPD was similar to that for H2O2. And the Km and Vmax values for oPD were calculated to be 71.69 mM and 2.06*10-7 M s−1, respectively (Fig. 3(f)). The kinetic parameters, determined using oPD as the substrate, were compared with previous studies (Table S1), the prepared Sn-CDs nanozyme possessed superior POD-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of AA and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The Sn-CDs nanozyme catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH, which could oxidize oPD to form ox-oPD with an absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. S4). Upon the addition of AA to the system, the AA and ox-oPD were oxidized and reduced to generate DHA and oPD, respectively, which led to the absorption at 450 nm disappear (Fig. S4). Hence, the AA concentration could be reflected at 450 nm, and the intensity increased with the increase of AA concentration. Meanwhile, the generated DHA with oxidizing ability could further react with oPD via Schiff base reaction to produce DFQ (Fig. S4) with an absorption at 336 nm, and its intensity increased with the increase of AA concentration (Fig. 4 (a)). Therefore, a dual-wavelength method (at 450 nm and 336 nm) for AA detection was developed. As shown in Fig. 4(b), at 450 nm, absorbance intensity was a linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 16.54–51.62 μM. And the linear equation was Y(450nm) = -0.73X + 0.32 (R2 = 0.994, limit of detection (LOD) = 6.88 μM). In Fig. 4(c), the increase in the intensity at 336 nm had a positive correlation with AA concentration. At lower concentrations of AA, the absorbance changes at 336 nm were linearly related to the concentration range of 5.62–131.03 μM. The corresponding linear regression equation for the absorbance at 336 nm was Y(336nm) = 5.73X-0.0026 (R2 = 0.993), and the calculated detection limit was 0.88 μM. Compared with the 450 nm results, the 336 nm results showed a higher linear range and a lower LOD. Interestingly, at high AA concentrations of millimoles per liter level, the absorbance changes at 336 nm were linear with the concentration of AA (2.84–28.39 mM) (Fig. 4(d)), the corresponding linear regression equation was Y(336nm) = 0.016X + 1.39 (R2 = 0.991). The performance of the constructed colorimetric sensor based on Sn-CDs nanozyme was compared with that of other studies, and the results were shown in Table S2. The typical linear range of AA detection by the CDs-based nanozyme colorimetric sensor was limited to 70 μM due to the high reductive ability of AA. In this study, the constructed colorimetric sensor based on Sn-CDs nanozyme demonstrated a higher detection range of 131.03 μM and even to 28.39 mM, which was higher than previous studies. In addition, the detection limit of the sensor was 0.88 μM, which was better than the reported values of 3.26 μM and 2.05 μM [28,31]. The above results approved that the constructed colorimetric sensor based on Sn-CDs nanozyme possessed excellent application prospect for the detection of AA.

The detection mechanism for AA based on the colorimetric sensor was investigated. In the initial stage, the Sn-CDs nanozyme catalyzed H2O2 to generate the •OH which could oxidize oPD to generate ox-oPD, which exhibited an absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. 5 (a), Fig. S4) and a fluorescence emission at 578 nm under excitation at 414 nm (Fig. S5). The fluorescence emission of ox-oPD was similar to the reported value of fluorescence emission at 570 nm [32]. And the mechanism diagram was shown in Fig. 5c. In the second stage, the AA was added into the system, the AA with reducing ability could react with the generated ox-oPD with oxidizing ability. The AA and ox-oPD were oxidized and reduced to generate DHA and oPD, respectively. Therefore, as the concentration of AA increased, more ox-oPD was reduced, which leads to absorption intensity at 450 nm decreased, and the process was shown in Fig. 5c and Fig. S4.

Meanwhile, the generated DHA with oxidizing ability could further react with oPD via Schiff base reaction to generated DFQ (Fig. S6), which exhibited an absorption peak at 336 nm (Fig. 5(a), Fig. S4) and a fluorescence emission at 432 nm under excitation at 375 nm (Fig. S6). The fluorescence emission of DFQ was in consistent with the published data at 440 nm [17]. And the process was shown in Fig. 5d. Hence, absorption intensity at 336 nm increased with the increase of AA concentration. There were the mechanisms for the 450 nm and 366 nm signals. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the signal at 336 nm could also appear in the system containing oPD + AA + H2O2, but the system containing oPD + AA had no 336 nm signal. This highlights that the DFQ could be formed under the presence of H2O2. We further detected the effects of Sn-CDs nanozyme in the system. As shown in Fig. S7, compared with the absorbance at the condition of without Sn-CDs nanozyme, the absorbance increased significantly at the condition of with Sn-CDs nanozyme, which indicated that the Sn-CDs nanozyme could promote the reaction of the system and accelerate the generation of DFQ. And when the concentration of Sn-CDs nanozyme was further increased, there was no significant change in the absorbance at 336 nm. The above phenomenon could be explained as the H2O2 as substrate concentration being constant and the CDs reacting with the substrate reaching equilibrium. Therefore, when Sn-CDs nanozyme exceeded a certain concentration, it caused the absorbance of 336 nm to no longer change significantly. This indicated that the Sn-CDs nanozyme could accelerate the reaction between DHA and oPD.

Various possible interfering agents, such as Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Al3+, NH4 +, glucose, glutathione, and glycine, were tested using this colorimetric method to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor for the detection of AA. When the absorption peak at 450 nm was used as the indicator, the test results of Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, glucose, glutathione, and glycine showed negligible effects on the AA detection (Fig. 5(e)). However, dopamine caused a decrease in absorbance at 450 nm as well as AA. This suggested that the selectivity of AA detection might be influenced by dopamine or other possible reductive agents. When the absorption peak at 336 nm was used as the indicator, the test results of all the possible interference agents showed negligible effects on the AA detection (Fig. 5(f)). This could be attributed to the specific reaction between oPD and DHA. In summary, the designed colorimetric sensor based on 336 nm absorption peak exhibited a higher anti-interfering ability in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the adaptability of the colorimetric sensor was explored in real samples including Vitamin C tablets, orange, and lemon. Table 1 showed the concentration of AA in the Vitamin C tablets, orange, and lemon via HPLC and constructed colorimetric sensor. For the Vitamin C tablet sample, the concentration of AA was 20.1 μM by colorimetric method, and the adding standard recovery of the colorimetric sensor was ranged from 90.15 % to 98.18 %, which indicated that the constructed colorimetric sensor for detection of AA possessed excellent accuracy. And the concentration of AA (22.6 μM) detected by HPLC further confirmed the superiority of the colorimetric method. To further approve the applicability of the colorimetric sensor, the concentrations of AA in orange and lemon samples were analyzed, and the concentrations were about 0.18 ± 0.020 mg g−1 (RSD: 4.51 %) and 0.13 ± 0.0061 mg g−1 (RSD: 1.94 %), respectively, which was consistent with the previous study [33]. Compared with the HPLC method, the contents in oranges and lemons were 0.19 mg g−1 and 0.15 mg g−1, respectively. Based on the analysis results, the concentrations of AA by the constructed colorimetric sensor were lower than that by the HPLC, which might be caused by the short detection time of HPLC led to less oxidation of AA by oxygen. The above results proved the feasibility of the constructed colorimetric sensor for detecting AA in practical samples.

The colorimetric sensor based on Sn-CDs nanozyme was successfully established, and it realized a wide range of AA detection. In detail, the Sn-CDs nanozyme with high enzymatic activity was prepared via the direct pyrolysis method. Based on the changes in absorption peaks at 450 nm and 336 nm, the colorimetric sensors with different detection ranges were established. For the 336 nm signal, Y(336nm) = 5.73X-0.0026 (R2 = 0.993). For the 450 nm signal, Y(450nm) = -0.73X + 0.32 (R2 = 0.994, LOD = 6.88 μM). Ox-oPD (450 nm) and DFQ (DHA reacting with oPD, 336 nm) served as an in-situ formed absorbance agent for colorimetric sensor development. And the colorimetric sensor possessed excellent accurate ability to detect AA in real samples. The constructed colorimetric sensor based on Sn-CDs nanozyme provided a new perspective for the detection of AA with a wide detection range and high selectivity.

Zhichao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xinghao Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Tongtong Chen: Data curation. Zhixuan Wang: Data curation. Zheng Xie: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2024QD168), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21875267, 22275202), and University of Jinan Disciplinary Cross-Convergence Construction Project 2023 (XKJC-202312) were acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114254.

ce:section.ce:para:
